data.title: A Look At Pan American Silver (TSX:PAAS) Valuation As La Colorada Skarn Plan Shifts To Lower Risk And Cost
data.description:
Pan American Silver (TSX:PAAS) has put its La Colorada Skarn project back in focus by releasing a revised economic assessment that shifts to a smaller, lower-risk mine plan with reduced upfront capital needs.

See our latest analysis for Pan American Silver.

The revised La Colorada plan lands at a time when Pan American Silver’s share price, at CA$79.4, has a 90 day share price return of 7.91% and a 1 year total shareholder return of 133.81%. This points to strong momentum after a mixed 30 day share price return of 3.48% and solid year to date share price gains of 13.35%.

If this kind of project update has you thinking more broadly about precious metals opportunities, it could be worth scanning other potential silver producers through our 8 top silver producer stocks

With Pan American Silver trading at CA$79.40 and recent returns already very strong, the key question now is simple: is the La Colorada reset still underappreciated, or has the market already priced in the company’s future growth?

Advertisement

Preferred P/E of 24.7x: Is it justified?

On simple valuation measures, Pan American Silver is not cheap right now, with a P/E of 24.7x compared with a peer average of 21.6x and a Canadian Metals and Mining industry average of 18.3x.

The P/E multiple compares the current share price to earnings per share, so a higher ratio usually reflects stronger earnings growth, higher perceived quality, or elevated expectations for future profitability. For a diversified precious metals producer that spans silver and gold across multiple jurisdictions, investors often pay closer attention to earnings power than to pure production volumes.

Recent fundamentals help explain why the market is prepared to pay up. Earnings have grown significantly over the past five years, and profit growth over the past year has been very large relative to both the company’s own history and the wider Metals and Mining industry. Net profit margins currently sit at 27%, compared with 4% last year, and earnings are forecast to grow faster than the broader Canadian market, even if the forecast rate is not in the very high growth bracket. That mix of stronger profitability, high quality earnings and faster expected earnings growth can support a premium to both the peer P/E and the estimated fair P/E of 24.4x, which is only slightly below where the market is pricing the stock today.

Compared with the Canadian Metals and Mining industry average P/E of 18.3x, Pan American Silver’s 24.7x ratio stands out as materially higher, suggesting investors are assigning a higher value to each dollar of earnings than they do for the typical name in the group. Against the peer average of 21.6x and an estimated fair P/E of 24.4x that the market could converge toward over time, the current multiple looks closely aligned with that implied fair level rather than obviously cheap.

data.title: Is Waste Management (WM) Fairly Priced After Recent Pullback In A Strong Multi Year Run?
data.description:
• Wondering if Waste Management at US$231.43 is offering fair value or asking you to pay up for its quality? This article walks through the key numbers so you can judge the price with more confidence.
  • The stock has returned 0.2% over the last 7 days and 6.0% year to date, with a 6.1% decline over the past month that contrasts with its 46.4% 3 year and 86.9% 5 year returns.
  • Recent attention on Waste Management has focused on its role as a core player in essential services and how that fits into portfolios during different market conditions. Broader coverage has also highlighted investor interest in established, cash generative companies. This helps frame how the recent price moves might be viewed.
  • Right now, Waste Management scores 2 out of 6 on our valuation checks. The next sections will compare common valuation approaches and then finish with a more complete way of thinking about what this price really means.

Waste Management scores just 2/6 on our valuation checks. See what other red flags we found in the full valuation breakdown.

Advertisement

Approach 1: Waste Management Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a business could be worth by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to today using a required rate of return. It is essentially asking what all those future dollars are worth in present terms.

For Waste Management, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach based on cash flow projections. The latest twelve month Free Cash Flow stands at about $2.56b. Analysts and extrapolated estimates point to projected Free Cash Flow of around $5.08b in 2035, with a series of forecasts between 2026 and 2035 that are discounted back to today in the model.

Bringing all those future cash flows into present dollars gives an estimated intrinsic value of $242.26 per share. Compared with the current share price of $231.43, the DCF output suggests the stock is trading at about a 4.5% discount to this estimate, which is a relatively small gap.

Result: ABOUT RIGHT

Waste Management is fairly valued according to our Discounted Cash Flow (DCF), but this can change at a moment's notice. Track the value in your watchlist or portfolio and be alerted on when to act.

Head to the Valuation section of our Company Report for more details on how we arrive at this Fair Value for Waste Management.

Approach 2: Waste Management Price vs Earnings

For profitable companies like Waste Management, the P/E ratio is a useful way to relate what you pay per share to the earnings that support that price. Higher expected earnings growth or lower perceived risk can justify a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually line up with a lower, more conservative multiple.

Waste Management currently trades on a P/E of 34.43x. That sits above the Commercial Services industry average P/E of 22.92x, but slightly below the peer group average of 35.89x. Simply Wall St also calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” of 30.23x for Waste Management, which reflects what the P/E might be given factors such as its earnings growth profile, industry, profit margins, market cap and company specific risks.

This Fair Ratio tends to be more tailored than a simple peer or industry comparison because it adjusts for differences in business quality and risk, rather than assuming all companies deserve similar multiples. With the current 34.43x P/E sitting above the 30.23x Fair Ratio, the shares screen as trading at a premium on this earnings based view.

Result: OVERVALUED

Earlier it was mentioned that there is an even better way to understand valuation. Narratives bring your view of Waste Management together in one place by linking the company story, your forecast for revenue, earnings and margins, and your own fair value, then comparing that fair value to the current price to help guide your decisions.

On Simply Wall St, Narratives sit inside the Community page and are used by millions of investors. They update automatically when new information such as news, earnings or guidance is added, so your story and numbers stay aligned with what is happening.

Another could worry more about tax credits, weather impacts, leverage and regulation and settle near the lower target of US$198.0. Approach 1: UnitedHealth Group Discounted Cash Flow (DCF) Analysis

A Discounted Cash Flow, or DCF, model estimates what a company could be worth today by projecting its future cash flows and discounting them back to a present value. It is essentially a way of translating tomorrow’s cash into today’s dollars.

For UnitedHealth Group, the model used is a 2 Stage Free Cash Flow to Equity approach, based on cash flow projections expressed in $. The latest twelve month free cash flow is about $15.8b. Analyst inputs and extrapolated estimates suggest projected free cash flow of $27.8b in 2030, with a series of ten year projections guiding how that path might look from 2026 through 2035.

For profitable companies, the P/E ratio is a useful quick check because it links what you pay for each share to the earnings that company is currently generating. It gives you a simple “price tag per dollar of earnings” that many investors use as a common reference point.

What counts as a normal or fair P/E often reflects how the market views a company’s growth potential and risk profile. Higher expected growth or lower perceived risk can support a higher P/E, while slower growth or higher risk usually lines up with a lower one.

P/E is a common way to value profitable companies because it links what you pay for each share directly to the earnings that each share generates. Investors usually accept a higher or lower P/E depending on what they expect for future earnings growth and how much risk they see in those earnings.

American Express currently trades on a P/E of 20.27x. That sits above the Consumer Finance industry average P/E of 8.48x, but slightly below the peer group average of 21.72x. On its own, that mix of numbers is hard to interpret because peers and industry may have very different growth profiles, margins and risk levels.

To refine this, Simply Wall St calculates a proprietary “Fair Ratio” for American Express of 18.78x. This is intended to be the P/E you might expect given factors such as earnings growth, profitability, industry, market cap and specific risks. Because it is tailored to the company rather than broad groups, it can be more informative than simple peer or industry comparisons. Set against the current P/E of 20.27x, American Express trades above this Fair Ratio. This points to the shares looking overvalued on this measure.

Result: OVERVALUED
